dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 responding for an unconscious/fainting patient at a Walmart pharmacy in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What occurred that made you call 911 today?
She fainted. She was on the floor, but she's awake now. We tried helping her up, but she was still dizzy.
I see. Can you tell me where you're feeling unwell? Is it in your head, chest, stomach, or something else?
It's in my head, just light-headedness, no fainting. But I need to lie down because I feel unstable when I stand.
Ma'am, how are you feeling right now? Can you tell me your name and the date today?
I'm Gloria, and we're at the Walmart pharmacy. I wasn't sure of the date at first, but now I remember after you told me.
I see. You opened your eyes spontaneously. You appeared fully alert during our introduction.
I would say a 4 now. Earlier, I was a bit groggy, maybe a 2 or 3, but I'm better now.
Got it. Can you tell me the time right now, please?
Great. Now, can you slowly squeeze my fingers one at a time-left hand first, then right?
You're breathing well, so let's proceed. We'll take some basic measurements to understand your condition. Can we check your blood pressure and oxygen now, please?
I'll begin with your blood pressure. Just a quick wrap around your arm. Are you currently on any medications for blood pressure or supplements right now?
Yes, I take lisinopril, and a couple of other pills every day. Let me show them in my bag-here are lisinopril, Celexa, Remeron, Percocet, Robaxin, Claritin, Motrin, and Pseudoephedrine.
Understood. First reading of your blood pressure is 148 over 96 with a heart rate of 79. Is there a time you've felt dizzy or light-headed before?
Your oxygen level is at 97%. Would it be okay to take your pulse manually on your wrist as well?
Your pulse is 79 and feels regular. Let's now do a quick blood sugar test. It's a simple finger prick. Is it okay to proceed?
Your blood sugar is 104 mg/dL. We'll repeat this later after some baseline evaluations.
Thank you. Now, I need to know more about your medical history. Do you have any ongoing medical conditions or diagnoses?

Yes, I have high blood pressure. I was also diagnosed with depression and am still on medication for it. Recently, I got back pain. I also have a history of schizoaffective disorder. I admit I have issues with alcohol-it's not daily, but it's a struggle.
I see. Let's connect you to a small heart monitor now. It's part of the process. Would you give me your consent for it?
We have a 4-lead ECG with a result showing sinus rhythm. Let's now perform a 12-lead for a full assessment. Is that okay with you?
Your oxygen levels look good and your breathing is steady at 19 per minute. We will keep an eye on it for now.
Your pulse is strong and your blood pressure is elevated but within a stable range. Do you have any type of pain currently?
No pain, really. It's just dizziness and instability. I feel like I might fall, but no actual discomfort.
Let's double-check that you're not showing any signs of shortness of breath or chest issues.
No shortness of breath or chest discomfort. I just feel lightheaded.
No signs of shortness of breath. Let's now check for any signs of elevated right-sided pressure due to chronic hypertension in your history.
Your blood pressure confirms chronic hypertension. No signs of acute changes or ST segment elevation. Let's keep that in the notes.
You seem to be afebrile. Let's document that.
We're now going to examine your head and neck. I'm using a light to check your eye movements. If you don't mind, I'll also examine your neck veins.
Let's now move to your back and limbs. I'll assist you to lie down onto the cot for this assessment.
On your current condition, when was the last time you had a meal or consumed any drinks?
I had some wine and malt liquor not long ago, but I don't remember the exact time-probably a couple of hours back.
On the second blood sugar test, the device read 0, but I think the first reading of 104 mg/dL was accurate as it was captured earlier in our notes. I'll log that and continue.
Let me check if you're showing any signs of airway compromise or breathing concerns. No distress noted. Just a moment, please.
Your breath sounds are symmetric and you're not in any distress. Your respiratory rate is currently 19-normal for you at rest.
No signs of airway obstruction or abnormal breathing. I also need to assess for any changes in heart conduction. Let's look at the EKG again.
The second 12-lead EKG interpreted as atypical finding, showing normal sinus rhythm. However, it also noted a short PR interval and reversed arm leads. I'll make a note of that.
Defined GCS score of 14 was confirmed-Eye 4, Verbal 4, Motor 6. No acute neurological decline noted at this time.
No signs of tension or dyspnea are present per the ePCR. Let's see any other signs or symptoms you may have.
I'll now assess your chest. No signs of shortness of breath as per the ePCR.
Your breathing remains steady. No abnormal heart sounds noted, and no signs of SOB.
Moving now to your abdomen and back area. I don't find any signs of signs of bowel complications or issues. Can you confirm there is no pain in your stomach or spine at this moment?
Yes, it feels flat. No pain anywhere in my stomach or spine at the moment.
I don't note any signs of genital or urinary symptoms. No increased frequency or urgency of urination. Let's check the lower extremities now.
Your extremities appear stable and no signs of swelling or wear. All pulses seem present and accounted for.
No signs of shortness of breath reported, consistent with the ePCR.
Per protocol 3-15, we're proceeding under altered mental status due to reported light-headedness and confusion possibly due to alcohol consumption. I'll log this and inform the paramedic.
We've decided to transport you to Retreat Hospital. It's an appropriate facility for your medical history and present condition. Is that acceptable?
Patient is secured on the cot and all emergency gear is in place. Everything appears stable.
Please let us know if at any time the dizziness returns or if you start to feel weak. We're here throughout the ride.
If you have any questions or notice a return of symptoms, don't hesitate to speak up. We monitor and adjust as needed.
Your vital signs at this point are stable, with a report of no significant pain as documented in the ePCR.
Prior to transport, I recorded a respiratory rate of 19. I'll note that in the records.
The final recorded pulse was 82-indicating a slight increase from the initial reading. I'll record that as well.
Your blood sugar remains steady at 104 mg/dL. I'll confirm there is no evidence of hypoglycemia.
No signs of shortness of breath. According to the ePCR, the patient has not experienced SOB.
Your blood pressure, 148/96, is on the higher side-borderline high, as noted in the ePCR.
Oxygen saturation is at 95%. Still within the normal range, and no hypoxic status reported in the ePCR.
No signs of shortness of breath reported, and your breathing remains stable at 19 breaths per minute.
Final blood pressure is 147 over 103, which is consistent with the patient's history of primary hypertension.
Let me know if you feel lightheaded or need any adjustments during transport.
Transport initiated. Vital signs monitored continuously.
